intro.kicker: Topic 7 of 10 • Chapter: Newton's Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: Concurrent forces all act at the same point. A body under concurrent forces is in equilibrium when the vector sum of all forces is zero: ΣF = 0 (i.e. ΣFx = 0 and ΣFy = 0). Three concurrent forces in equilibrium can be represented by the three sides of a triangle taken in order. Lami's Theorem provides an elegant ratio-based solution: F₁/sinα = F₂/sinβ = F₃/sinγ. High-yield for NEET inclined plane, hanging mass, and FBD problems.

expectedQuestions.description: Direct equilibrium condition MCQs and Lami's Theorem applications on three-force systems (suspended masses, angled ropes). FBD problems implicitly use this topic throughout Newton's Laws.

timeRequired.description: Cover concurrent forces definition, equilibrium condition (vector sum = 0), triangle rule for three forces, and Lami's Theorem. Lami's Theorem is the highest-yield subtopic.

difficulty.description: Equilibrium condition is conceptually easy. Lami's Theorem application requires correct angle assignment — the angles between forces (not from axes) — a common NEET trap.

nriUsaGap.description: US AP Physics covers force equilibrium via component resolution. Lami's Theorem is not in the US curriculum — it is NEET/NCERT-specific and directly tested.

weightage.sectionTitle: Equilibrium of Concurrent Forces — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Lami's Theorem is tested nearly every cycle — know the formula and angle convention precisely.
ΣFx = 0 and ΣFy = 0 conditions underpin all static equilibrium problems including inclined planes with strings.
Triangle law for three concurrent forces in equilibrium — visually tested in NEET diagram MCQs.

strategySteps.title: Master the equilibrium conditions
strategySteps.description: ΣFx = 0 and ΣFy = 0 simultaneously. For 3D problems: ΣFz = 0 as well. These are the necessary and sufficient conditions.

strategySteps.title: Apply Lami's Theorem correctly
strategySteps.description: F₁/sinα = F₂/sinβ = F₃/sinγ where α, β, γ are the angles BETWEEN THE OTHER TWO FORCES (not the angle a force makes with an axis). Draw the force diagram and label inter-force angles.

strategySteps.title: Use the triangle rule for 3 forces
strategySteps.description: Three concurrent forces in equilibrium can be represented by the three sides of a triangle taken in order. If you create the vector polygon it closes — yielding the equilibrium condition geometrically.

cards.title: Equilibrium of Concurrent Forces — Full Notes
cards.description: Concurrent force definition, equilibrium conditions, triangle law, Lami's Theorem with derivation and NEET numerical examples.

cards.title: Lami's Theorem Problems Pack
cards.description: 15 solved problems on three-force equilibrium using Lami's Theorem — hanging masses, knotted strings, angled pulls.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Equilibrium of Concurrent Forces

cards.title: Concurrent Forces — Definition
cards.coreRevision: If all the forces working on a body are acting on the same point, they are said to be concurrent. A body under concurrent forces is in equilibrium when said state (rest/uniform motion) does not change.
points: Concurrent = all force lines of action pass through one point
Equilibrium: no change in state of rest or uniform motion
Necessary condition: vector sum of all forces = 0
cards.example: A traffic light hanging from two cables and gravity forms three concurrent forces at the junction point. In static equilibrium, the three forces balance to ΣF = 0.

cards.title: Equilibrium Conditions
cards.coreRevision: For equilibrium: ΣF_net = 0, which means ΣFx = 0 and ΣFy = 0 (and ΣFz = 0 in 3D). These are the necessary and sufficient conditions for concurrent force equilibrium.
points: Resolve all forces into x and y components
Set net force in each direction to zero
Solve the resulting equations for unknown forces or angles
cards.example: A block on an inclined plane held by a horizontal string: resolve W (weight), N (normal), T (tension) along and perpendicular to plane. Set ΣF‖ = 0 and ΣF⊥ = 0 to find T and N.

cards.title: Triangle Law for Three Forces
cards.coreRevision: Three concurrent forces in equilibrium can be represented completely by the three sides of a triangle taken in order. The triangle closes (no resultant) — reflecting ΣF = 0.
points: Draw the forces head-to-tail as triangle sides
If the triangle closes, the forces are in equilibrium
This is a geometric equivalent of the vector sum = 0 condition
cards.example: Forces of 3 N, 4 N, and 5 N can form a 3-4-5 right triangle. If oriented correctly head-to-tail, they are in equilibrium.

cards.title: Lami's Theorem
cards.coreRevision: For three concurrent coplanar forces in equilibrium: F₁/sin α = F₂/sin β = F₃/sin γ, where α, β, γ are the angles between the OTHER two forces (not from the axes).
points: α = angle between F₂ and F₃; β = between F₃ and F₁; γ = between F₁ and F₂
Use when you know angles between forces — faster than resolving components
cards.example: A mass hangs from two strings making 30° and 60° with the vertical. Using Lami's Theorem on the junction point (three forces: T₁, T₂, and weight W) gives T₁ and T₂ directly without resolving components.

curriculum.hintText: Key differences in equilibrium treatment between NEET and US AP Physics.

gaps.title: Lami's Theorem
gaps.description: Not part of US AP Physics curriculum. NEET tests it directly with numerical MCQs.
bullets: Memorise the formula: F₁/sinα = F₂/sinβ = F₃/sinγ
Crucial: angles α, β, γ are between force pairs, not with coordinate axes
Faster than component resolution for three-force problems

gaps.title: Triangle Law for Forces
gaps.description: US AP uses the polygon method generally; NEET explicitly tests the triangle rule as a named theorem for three concurrent equilibrium forces.
bullets: Three concurrent equilibrium forces form a closed triangle
Diagram MCQs test whether a set of forces (given as triangle sides) is in equilibrium

pyq.mcqCountLabel: Selected MCQs — Equilibrium of Concurrent Forces

questions.question: Three concurrent forces in equilibrium can be represented completely by:

options.text: Three sides of a triangle taken in order
Three sides of a triangle taken in any order
Two sides and the diagonal of a parallelogram

questions.explanation: By Lami's theorem and the triangle law: three concurrent coplanar forces in equilibrium can be represented completely by the three sides of a triangle taken in ORDER (not random order — direction matters for vector representation).

questions.question: A body is in equilibrium under three concurrent forces F₁, F₂, and F₃. The angle between F₁ and F₂ is 120°, and between F₂ and F₃ is 130°. The angle between F₃ and F₁ is:
questions.explanation: In Lami's theorem, the sum of the three angles between force pairs = 360°. So: 120° + 130° + α = 360° → α = 110°.

questions.question: The necessary condition for the equilibrium of a body under concurrent forces is:
In Lami's theorem for three concurrent forces F₁, F₂, F₃, the constant ratio is:
Forces acting on a body are concurrent if:
options: They all pass through the same point
They are perpendicular to each other
questions.answer: They all pass through the same point

questions.question: In the equilibrium condition ΣFx = 0 and ΣFy = 0, the sum of angles in Lami's theorem is:

items.title: Define concurrent forces
items.description: All force lines of action pass through the same point.

items.title: State equilibrium conditions
items.description: ΣFx = 0, ΣFy = 0 (and ΣFz = 0 in 3D). Vector sum of all forces = 0.

items.title: Apply triangle law
items.description: Three concurrent equilibrium forces form a closed triangle when drawn head-to-tail.

items.title: Master Lami's Theorem
items.description: F₁/sinα = F₂/sinβ = F₃/sinγ; angles between force pairs (α + β + γ = 360°). Not with axes.

items.title: Practice three-force systems
items.description: Hanging masses, knotted strings, particles on inclines — all are three-force equilibrium problems solvable via Lami's Theorem.

items.question: What is the difference between equilibrium of concurrent forces and general equilibrium?
items.answer: General equilibrium requires both ΣF = 0 (translational equilibrium) AND Στ = 0 (rotational equilibrium, torques about any axis). For concurrent forces (all acting at one point), torques about the point of concurrency are automatically zero — so only ΣF = 0 is needed. Concurrent force equilibrium is a simpler special case.

items.question: When should I use Lami's Theorem vs. component resolution?
items.answer: Use Lami's Theorem when exactly three concurrent forces act and you know the angles between the forces (or need to find them). It gives the answer in one step using the sine rule. Use component resolution (ΣFx = 0, ΣFy = 0) when forces are not all known, when more than three forces act, or when the geometry is more easily expressed in Cartesian coordinates.

items.question: Can a body in equilibrium be moving?
items.answer: Yes. Translational equilibrium means zero net force → zero acceleration → constant velocity. A body in uniform motion (constant speed in a straight line) satisfies ΣF = 0 and is in dynamic equilibrium. Static equilibrium is the special case where velocity = 0 as well.

grounding_summary: Page 192 defines concurrent forces (all acting on the same point) and states the equilibrium condition: no change in state of rest or uniform motion. The necessary condition is that the vector sum of all forces must be zero (ΣFx = 0, ΣFy = 0). Three concurrent forces in equilibrium can be represented by three sides of a triangle taken in order. Lami's Theorem is presented: F₁/sinα = F₂/sinβ = F₃/sinγ.
grounding_excerpt: If all the forces working on a body are acting on the same point, then they are said to be concurrent.
A body, under the action of concurrent forces, is said to be in equilibrium, when there is no change in the state of rest or of uniform motion along a straight line.
The necessary condition for the equilibrium of a body under the action of concurrent forces is that the vector sum of all the forces acting on the body must be zero.
